transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hey, Mark.
Recently added a Zoom link.
Sharing here as well for use of use.
Hey, Mark.
Hey, how are you?
Good.
How are you?
I'm good.
How about you?
Well, doing pretty good.
I'm just kind of going through my week here.
I figured it was kind of a bummer that I had to send the non-renewal notice yesterday.
I kind of pushed it out as far as I could to work with, like, you know, Mark and the CEO to make a decision.
But I wanted to, you know, get on with you guys and, you know, kind of be able to give you all an explanation of why we weren't moving forward and answer any questions that you had and all that.
Yeah, I really appreciate that.
Thank you for.
Thank extending an offer.
Really appreciate it.
We're bummed, of course.
Would love to continue working with you.
I have a few questions, but if you don't mind maybe just sharing a little bit of context about what led to the decision, maybe any learnings that we can use in the future would be really helpful.
Yeah.
So part of it is just kind of are pushing on where budgets are being allocated.
So I think he wants to, he's seeing a lot of success in events and like, like in particular physical events.
So like going to trade shows, putting on our own events.
And he's like seeing all these sales coming from that.
And then he's not seeing a lot of sales coming from like digital marketing efforts or a lot of conversions coming from what we're getting online.
So he's looking at it and going, why are we spending our money over here?
I want you to spend more money over here.
So I would have had to have made a very compelling case for why we would.
Keep doing it.

And, you know, we're not seeing the results that I would need to be able to show to be able to say, like, keep this budget over here instead of spending 30 grand on this big event in Europe next year.
And so we're getting to the end of the year.
We're doing that kind of exercise.
And that's that's kind of how it shook out.
So that's I mean, that's that's the long part of it is just he's looking at where money is making the biggest impact and didn't see this as making making as big of an impact as other things.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Yeah.
And really appreciate you being so transparent.
Were there any like early internal indicators or messaging that like we should be asking about or aware of in the future just to help like kind of forecast this out a little bit?
Well, I think I've been all the things that I've talked to you about, I think, are some of the.
reasons why we weren't able to get the ROI.
I do think that like towards the end here, I still think that there's a value to these country pages and the versus pages.
So I'm pushing my team, like get as many of these done as we can before the end of December.
Cause I do think there's, there is a value in those still.
But I did spend a little bit at the time.
I spent a little bit of my time prior to the call, just thinking through why I think it wasn't working out.
And I can share that with you.
That'd be great.
Yeah.
So I think the first thing is the length and complexity of the blog posts puts a lot of puts a, puts a lot of work on the team.

Now it does put less work on the team than if we were to write this many blog posts, but it actually ends up putting more work on the team than if we just went back to like the traditional strategy of like, ah, get out one or two posts a week.
And first thing.
Mm-hmm.
And write it yourself, right?
So it's kind of like, it doesn't actually reduce workload, and it doesn't justify increasing our headcount at this point.
So it was hard to maintain the scale.
Some things that I think are worth considering with that is, you know, maybe, I know why you do these long blog posts, because in a lot of use cases, they probably, you know, do better.
For SEO purposes, I've heard long blog posts do.
But if I look at our top traffic driving posts, right now, I just went from like August to November.
Yeah.
Some of these are with you guys, you know, things that we have either updated, like this Credit Card Processing 101 or Best Crypto Gateways, or like, you know, new posts that we've created.
But some of them are also posts that we've created ourselves.
you know, like, to ЧDD, I Yeah.
Yeah.
And this

Of those posts that we've created ourselves, like, for instance, this Merchant Category Code MCC3799, Business Services, otherwise classified, or maybe more relevant, this, like, Travel Agency Services post.
Yeah.
Like, we get a lot of traffic off of this, and I think part of the reason why is it's really targeted to that industry.
Like, if you're looking for Travel Agency Services, you'd go look at it.
But the post is really short.
You know, it just happens to be, like, super hyper-focused, but then that also makes it really easy to write a review.
So it's, like, quick explanation of what it is, like, a scope, and that's it.
So it's, you know, it's probably, like, a page and a half in a Word doc.
And there's nothing in it that's, like, new or interesting or, like, kind of pushing.
It's just, like, kind of taking information that already exists and just sticking it all in one place and putting it online.
All right.
All
With the right keyword at the top.
And that type of post I get, it's not quite what we're doing, but it's a heck of a lot easier to like review and to get these out quickly.
And we pushed out a lot of those.
For the payment method posts that we're doing, like, if I Google like top payment methods in Brazil, Audien comes up number one.
And, you know, you look at them, obviously Audien's a bigger company than us, but you look at the post and it's like, it doesn't even tell you the top payment methods.
It's like a paragraph, population and currency, and then like a card for five payment methods, and that's it.

And so we could, from just a content standpoint, we could just beat this by getting some data for each one of these and saying like 50% of online transactions and 20% of in-person transactions are PICs.
You know, like if just plus this up a little bit, have a few paragraphs and like maybe some top industries in Brazil.
So, and then it's easier for us to review and easier to publish.

So I think that length and complexity was difficult because if it's not kind of just repurposing the same stuff that everybody else is saying, we lack the expertise to actually review it and know if it's accurate.
So that it's like a catch-22.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That makes sense.
So that was one of the challenges.
Yeah.
I think overall, too, we just kind of struggled a little bit to find the voice.
And that, I think that goes back to the workload question.
You know, it's great to have AI that can automate things and simplify them for you.
But we, you know, I would need to spend quite a bit of time, say, really diving into those inquiries in Scrunch to make sure that we're searching for the right thing for that to be useful.
Really working on the personas with you and dialing those in to make sure that those are useful in the artifacts.
And all of that stuff, again, like, takes time.
And in something that you're using to try to take time off or give yourself time back.
I can automate something.
We just didn't have like, we struggled to have the headcount and the capacity to manage it.
Yeah.
Can you remind me, did Rapyd go through the sprint phase or did that happen?
We were kind of early coming in and I think that it wasn't fully baked when we did it.
So we did something, but I don't think it was elaborate as what you do now.
Like my impression is like, once you got to Atlas, you kind of started doing stuff in a more sophisticated way.
Okay.

That's helpful because my, like hearing this, just thinking of like what we can do to change so that if we're working with someone like Rapyd in the future, like we can avoid the like hassle of reviews for you.

And also just dial in a little bit earlier so that we can enjoy the downstream impact would be sprint phase, probably answering some of those really in-depth questions and then doing all that architecture together.
So it's, it's a limited amount of time that you have to spend really like answering.

answering those questions and then it's easy reviews from there and also I'm thinking maybe just based on the industry we should consider length and also originality to a degree and maybe that's something we want to consider for like ratio of content that we produce just to be mindful of your time and internal expertise.
So this is all really valuable.
Is there anything else that you had on your list that we should know?

Yeah, this has nothing to do with your products or what you delivered, but just from a budget deployment standpoint, again, SEO and GEO is changing so much that if I were to get back the 120 grand or so, I might consider deploying it on a full-time person as opposed to a platform because I don't just need blogs.
I need somebody who can like go figure out if our site schema is optimized and optimize that and go look at pages and create like map a keyword to page strategy and go off site and up.
Our G2 reviews and our SiteForge reviews and our About page on LinkedIn and whatever, you know, that scope is expanding.
And so, again, like that's not a criticism of your business.
It's just if like I feel that the need has changed for me from blogging a little bit to maybe like somebody who knows broadly what needs to be managed from a SEO, GEO standpoint.

And the only takeaway that I would say that that's applicable there for you is in knowing if a company is a good fit, maybe having that person in place already could be a good clue for you that they have the maybe capability, capacity, and discipline to fully leverage what you're doing.
Yeah.

And in terms of like schema, site audits, optimization requests, if that was something that was kind of rolled into what we were doing, I'm even just thinking of like product roadmap, if that's something that's feasible for us to build.
Without a...
Would been enough of an incentive to stay?
Like if we were a little bit more full service in that sense, or would you still kind of want someone in-house?
In our particular circumstance here where my CEO is saying, hey, we're making a bunch of money with events and not making any money with blogs, it wouldn't have made a difference because of that.
Overall, if we were seeing results with you, it's something that I would consider outsourcing as opposed to insourcing.
Okay.
And are you able to share that before?
Yeah.

Are you able to share like anything like quantitative of the delta between what you're seeing in events in person and just to get a sense of like maybe the benchmark we would have needed to clear, you know, to be competitive?
Yeah.
All our sales are coming from events.
Right.
Okay.
That's the problem.
Considerable.
Got it.
Okay, cool.
But honestly, it's like, it's, it's, it's where we're seeing results.
And some of that could be the type of business that we are.
There are lots of B2B businesses where the sale starts as a Google search infrastructure company.
So maybe when you get to that infrastructure level, maybe things are just a little different.
Yeah.

And is there, I understand if you don't have an immediate answer to this, but I'm just thinking of, A, is this like, it sounds like final decision, but is there any wiggle room on if we did like a pilot period or a pause or something where we could regain your trust and that investment?
Or it seems like because it's 100% events, is there basically like, is there a threshold for what you'd be paying where it might be worth it?
And I could go back and advocate for a lower fee in some way or?
Not at this point, because the decision has come from the CEO.
The legal team sent me a note to send out.
They've moved on to, like, they've kind of made their decisions.
It would, it's unlikely I could get anybody to change their decision now.
Yeah.
Okay.
Understood.
Okay.
This is all helpful.
And let us, like, kind of think about this.
And maybe there's a point where in the future, like, we can be a little bit of a more valuable partner, maybe at a different stage of business, or just, like, you know, maybe there's another product.
We'll keep it in mind.
And if it's all right, we'll reach out to you if something, if we build something, or we feel like we might be more valuable in the future.
I mean, here's the one place where that, here's where I could see that calculus changing.
There's a certain personal budget that I just kind of have authority over, right?
Like, there's a certain point where I can just swipe a credit card and do things.
And I do this all the time.
It's like, oh, I need a transcription, an AI transcription service.
I just swipe a credit card and get an AI transcription service.
It's 100 bucks a month, 500 bucks a month, whatever.
I know right now you're a services company, but you're building a platform out.
If this ever became the kind of thing where it's like, you can just have access to the platform, can load up your keywords, you can build your blogs on demand.
We're not touching it.
Your team's managing it.
And it's a couple hundred bucks a month or even a thousand bucks a month or whatever.
It's just a different discussion and a different budget at that point that it comes out of.
That makes perfect sense.
I was going to ask you and that I can understand how that would be a better use case for you.
Okay, great.
Well, thank you.
This has been so helpful.
Vivek, do you have any follow-up questions or anything?
I don't want to monopolize too much if there's something specific you're wondering.
Nothing major.
Just one question in terms of the content quality slash fit.
Was there ever a moment where you felt like, okay, this is declining, or was it consistently kind of not quite there?
It actually noticeably declined when you switched over.
So I felt like it was always kind of hard to, you know, the blogs were long, they're expensive, they were complex.
I know Atlas was supposed to be something like an improvement for you, but from my perspective, the content actually declined when you switched over in platforms.
And then I would say recently, it's gotten maybe back up to where it was, but that's where I noticed a decline.
That's helpful.
Can I ask a question back?
Sorry.
Here you go.
I would just be curious, what were the specific things that you noticed, like the themes, when we switched to Atlas, that definitely just like nose dived?
So the most noticeable, I didn't know why it was doing this until we looked, but we had these like marketplace focused personas in there.
And so the most noticeable thing when we went to Atlas was that we were just hyper focusing on things that didn't matter.
And then when I saw like, oh, we have these personas, or we've decided that marketplaces are very important.
That's why it's doing this.
So that was one of them.
And then I think with the compliance and the token.
Organization.
There are some other things like that, too, where it just sort of honed in on a few things that didn't matter that much and made them very important.
Yep.
Okay.
That's very helpful.
Sorry, Vivek.
Feel free to...
No, that was my question, pretty much.
Yeah.
Makes sense.
Anything else you're curious about?
Nope.
That's it.
Yeah.
Great.
Well, I don't want to take all of your time, Mark, but we've loved working with you, so we're sorry.
Not to get to have weekly syncs with you, but we also want to be, like, as optimally useful to you before our partnership closes in December.

So if there's anything that you're like, I think this is well-suited to the work that we've been doing together, and we'll also come with recommendations, but if there's anything that's really important to you that we deliver by the end of or beginning of December, we'll prioritize that.
So just give us a heads up.
Okay.
I mean, we've got a lot of blogs in the hopper now.
I'm going to prioritize countries and versus posts with my team.
If we prioritize countries and versus posts with your team, I think that's what we can focus on between now and December 9th or wherever the end of the engagement is, and we'll be fine.
Okay, great.
I think we should get those out over the next couple of weeks.
We're rewriting some of those just based on our discussion yesterday.
So, yeah, we'll start getting those out to you next week.
Okay.
Great.
Okay.
Well, thank you so much, Mark.
Just really appreciate you, like, offering to sync with us, taking the time to, like, think through what could be improved and share some feedback.
Like, it's really helpful.
So I appreciate it.
Great.
Well, I hope you all have a good rest of your day.
Yeah, you too.
Okay, we'll see you next week.
Thanks so much.
Thanks, Mark.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[To understand the reasons for Rapyd's non-renewal of the GrowthX contract.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=31.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Budget Reallocation:** Rapyd's CEO is shifting all marketing budget from digital to physical events (trade shows, proprietary events), which are now the sole source of new sales.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=67.0)
  - [**Content Strategy Mismatch:** Long, complex blog posts created a review bottleneck for Rapyd's team, who lacked the expertise to validate them. This was less efficient than their own successful strategy of publishing short, hyper-focused posts.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=209.0)
  - [**Platform-Induced Decline:** Content quality declined after the Atlas platform launch, which caused a hyper-focus on irrelevant topics (e.g., marketplaces) due to misconfigured personas.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=885.0)
  - [**Future Opportunity:** A low-cost, self-serve platform (e.g., $1k/mo) could be a fit for Mark's personal budget, but a full-service agency is not currently needed.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=818.0)

## Topics

### Primary Driver: Budget Reallocation

  - [The non-renewal was a CEO-level decision to reallocate all marketing budget from digital to physical events.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=67.0)
  - [**Rationale:** Events are the only source of new sales, while digital marketing shows no measurable ROI.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=78.0)
  - [**Outcome:** Mark could not justify the \~$120k annual contract against the CEO's directive to fund a major European trade show.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=112.0)

### Secondary Driver: Content Strategy Mismatch

  - [The core issue was a review bottleneck caused by the length and complexity of GrowthX's blog posts.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=200.0)
  - [**Problem:** Rapyd's team lacked the subject matter expertise to validate the accuracy of complex content, making the review process slower and more resource-intensive than writing their own posts.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [**Contrast:** Rapyd's own successful strategy involves publishing short, hyper-focused posts (e.g., "MCC3799," "Travel Agency Services").](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=254.0)
      - [**Why it works:** Easy to write, quick to review, and highly effective for targeted traffic.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=299.0)
      - [**Opportunity:** GrowthX could beat competitors (e.g., Adyen) by adding simple data points to short posts, making them more valuable and easier to review.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=371.0)

### Platform & Process Issues

  - [**Content Quality Decline:** Content quality noticeably declined after the Atlas platform launch.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=885.0)
      - [**Cause:** Misconfigured personas (e.g., marketplace-focused) led to a hyper-focus on irrelevant topics.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=897.0)
      - [**Recovery:** Quality has since improved to pre-Atlas levels.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=897.0)
  - [**Process Bottleneck:** Rapyd lacked the internal capacity to manage the GrowthX process effectively.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=443.0)
      - [**Requirement:** Effective use of tools like Scrunch and detailed persona work requires significant time investment.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=443.0)
      - [**Constraint:** This time investment was not possible without additional headcount.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=443.0)

### Future Partnership Opportunity

  - [**Current Need:** Rapyd's need has evolved from blogging to broad SEO management (site schema, keyword mapping, off-site optimization).](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=567.0)
  - [**Ideal Solution:** A full-time, in-house SEO specialist.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=567.0)
  - [**Platform Opportunity:** A low-cost, self-serve platform could be a fit for Mark's personal budget authority (e.g., $100–$1,000/mo).](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=818.0)
      - [**Why it works:** This model shifts the cost to a different budget category, making it a viable option.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=818.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**GrowthX:** Prioritize delivery of all "country" and "versus" posts before the contract ends on December 9.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=990.0)
  - [**GrowthX:** Use this feedback to refine its product and service model, focusing on:](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
      - [A more robust sprint phase for early alignment.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
      - [Offering a mix of content types (short vs. long) to match client capacity.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
      - [Exploring a low-cost, self-serve platform model.](https://fathom.video/share/PK3EsyCWWBnnZDN8GDGw72VKzei3sT7e?tab=summary&timestamp=818.0)

action_items.description: Deliver country and versus posts to Mark for review/publish; prioritize next 2 wks
